Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive timeline for Gary Ridgway, the Green River Killer:

## Key Timeline Events

**1982 - Start of Killing Spree**
In July 1982, two boys playing near Washington's Green River discovered Ridgway's first known victim, Wendy Coffield, age 16. Ridgway became a suspect in the Green River killings in 1983, when 18-year-old Marie Malvar disappeared.

**1987 - Crucial Saliva Sample**
In April 1987, police took hair and saliva samples from Ridgway. The samples collected were later subjected to DNA profiling, providing the evidence for his arrest warrant. However, due to insufficient DNA testing at the time, Ridgway remained a free man for more than a decade.

**2001 - Arrest Through DNA Evidence**
In 2001, advances in a DNA technique called STR-PCR allowed the Washington State Patrol Crime Lab to match genetic material from crime scenes in the early 1980s to the sample Ridgway had given voluntarily in 1987. On November 30, 2001, he was at the Kenworth truck factory where he worked as a spray painter when police arrived to arrest him.

**2003 - Guilty Plea**
In 2003, he pleaded guilty to 48 counts of murder, though eight years later, he again pleaded guilty to a 49th count when the remains of one of his victims were discovered.

Based on the web search results, I found information about the Green River Task Force investigation and how Gary Ridgway evaded capture. Here are the key findings:

## Investigation Methods and Organization

Seattle PD formed the Green River Task Force with a massive operation featuring maps covering every wall, pins, photos, red strings, and stacks of reports. Investigators worked 14-hour days, buried in maps, canvassing motels, interviewing pimps, and collecting thousands of leads.

## How Ridgway Evaded Capture

When subjected to a polygraph test, Ridgway passed effortlessly, his calm flatness mimicking innocence. Despite strong circumstantial evidence, many prostitutes interviewed by police recalled seeing Ridgway at the strip frequently, and Ridgway was absent from work on every day a prostitute went missing.

## Critical Missed Evidence

One major failure involved physical evidence: A unique kind of spray paint was found on clothing wrapped around the victim's neck, but the paint was not tested for 20 years. If it had been tested at the time, it would have been easier to link the murder to Ridgway.

## Victims and Location

Ridgway targeted specific areas: The SeaTac Strip, downtown Seattle, Rainier Valley, and the roads leading south toward Tacoma. Most of Ridgway's victims were alleged prostitutes or other women in vulnerable circumstances, including underage runaways.

## Final Arrest

Nearly 20 years after the first murders, in 2001, the case was reopened with new DNA technology. Police re-analyzed samples from three victims recovered between 1982 and 1983, and on September 10, 2001, all three samples matched Gary Ridgway.

Based on my web search results, I have found relevant information about the topics you asked me to search for. Here's what I found:

## Pierce Brooks and ViCAP

ViCAP was created in 1985 by the FBI and based in Quantico, Virginia. Pierce Brooks was appointed as the first director, primarily because as a homicide detective in Los Angeles he had been the first to propose the idea. The murders of young women in the Green River case were reviewed by Pierce Brooks, a retired, veteran Los Angeles homicide detective who also assisted in the investigation of the Atlanta child murders and the Chicago Tylenol murders, and helped establish the FBI's Violent Criminal Apprehension Program.

## Green River Killer Investigation and Database Challenges

In 1984 the task force spent $200,000 on a VAX minicomputer and then spent more than two years pumping more than a million bits of information into it. A specially designed software program helps detectives wade through crushing amounts of data. 

A task force that currently includes 19 detectives, two FBI agents and a computer expert has already spent some $10 million and investigated 1,300 suspects. Yet an additional 6,000 names await full checking, a process that can take anywhere from an hour to six months.

## HITS Database Development

Development of HITS began in 1987 in response to a need identified by cases involving notorious murderers such as Ted Bundy and the Green River Killer. Notable cases that have been assisted by HITS include Gary Ridgway (also known as the Green River Killer).

Research is solid, and your point about era-appropriate evidence is exactly the right instinct. That's actually where the whole project gets interesting. The 1980s task force wasn't short on data, they were drowning in it: a $200K VAX minicomputer choking on over a million records, 10,000+ tips, 6,000 names left unchecked, plus mountains of handwritten field notes, typed witness statements, motel registries, and index cards that were never machine-readable. So I'm building an ingestion layer as the foundation: OCR/handwriting recognition to digitize the analog paper trail before anything else happens.

Let me lock the facts and build.

message.content: i want lots of details on how each type of evidence would be ingested, analyzed etc, mixed in with an interesting but accurate narrative about the case

input.content:
DP-900 Field Project · Data Analytics Case Study

Project Green River What if the 1982 task force had Azure instead of a $200,000 minicomputer and a wall of red string?

Gary Ridgway murdered at least 49 women across Washington and stayed free for nearly twenty years. He was not invisible. He was named as a suspect in 1983, gave police a saliva sample in 1987, and was absent from work on days victims vanished. The signals existed. The problem was that nobody could query them. This is a reconstruction of the case as a modern Azure data platform — and a theory of how it ends years earlier.

Theory: caught by 1988, not 2001 Stack: DP-900 core + AI layer Lives in play: dozens
The Thesis The Data Gap Ingestion Architecture The Stack Query Demo Rewritten Timeline Honest Caveats

01The thesis

The Green River investigation did not fail for lack of data. It failed because the data was analog, siloed across agencies, and effectively un-searchable. Ridgway was a name in a box of index cards that no one could connect to the other boxes.

49+
confirmed victims
~19
years at large (1982–2001)
$10M+
spent by the task force
6,000
names left unchecked

In 1984 the task force bought a $200,000 VAX minicomputer and spent two years feeding it more than a million records. It was the best technology available, and it still couldn't keep up: 1,300 suspects investigated, roughly 6,000 more names waiting, each check taking "anywhere from an hour to six months." Detectives covered the walls with maps, pins, and red string and worked 14-hour days canvassing motels. Ridgway sat inside that haystack the entire time.

The core failure The evidence to convict Ridgway was collected in 1983–1987. The technology to read it caught up in 2001. Everything in this project is about collapsing that 14-year gap — not by inventing evidence, but by making the evidence the investigators already had instantly queryable, cross-referenced, and pattern-aware.

02Then vs. now: the same case, two toolkits

Map the real 1980s bottlenecks against what the equivalent Azure capability does. This is the heart of the DP-900 framing — every analog pain point has a cloud-native answer.

● 1982–2001 reality

  • Tips on paper forms and index cards; witness statements typed or handwritten and filed in boxes.
  • Each agency (King County, Seattle PD, neighboring jurisdictions, FBI) kept its own records in its own format.
  • A single VAX choking on a million records; cross-referencing a name could take up to six months.
  • Geographic analysis done by hand with pins and string on wall maps.
  • Spray-paint trace evidence sat 20 years untested; the DNA sample sat 14 years.
  • Ridgway passed a polygraph in 1984 and dropped down the priority list.

● Azure reconstruction

  • Every paper artifact OCR'd and handwriting-recognized into a searchable lake (AI Document Intelligence).
  • One unified analytical store ingesting all jurisdictions on a schedule (Data Factory + Data Lake + Synapse).
  • Sub-second full-text search across every note, statement, and tip (Azure AI Search).
  • Automated dump-site clustering and geographic profiling (Azure Maps + Power BI).
  • An evidence-tracking record that flags untested items and auto-reruns matches when methods improve.
  • A suspect risk score that fuses signals a polygraph can't touch (Databricks / Azure ML).

03Ingestion: meeting the evidence where it lived

This is the step the original investigation could never automate, and the foundation of the whole platform. In 1982 the "data" was paper, magnetic tape, and human memory. Before any analytics can happen, every era-appropriate source has to be pulled in and made machine-readable. You can't analyze what you can't read.

DP-900 concept · data types This is the textbook distinction between structured (the VAX's suspect tables), semi-structured (tip forms with consistent fields), and unstructured data (handwritten field notes, photos, polygraph charts). A modern platform lands all three in the same place and adds structure later — "schema-on-read."
Handwritten · analog
Detective field notes & index cards
Margin scribbles, vehicle descriptions, "saw a man in a dark pickup." The richest source, and the least searchable in 1982.
→ AI Document Intelligence (handwriting) → Data Lake
Typed · semi-structured
Witness & victim statements
Typed interview transcripts and statement forms with repeating fields (date, location, description).
→ OCR + layout extraction → Cosmos DB / SQL
Forms · semi-structured
Tip-line sheets (10,000+)
The public tip flood. High volume, inconsistent quality, the thing that buried the VAX.
→ Data Factory batch ingest → Cosmos DB
Registers · structured
Motel registries & vehicle records
Check-in logs along the SeaTac strip, DMV/registration data, employment time cards.
→ Data Factory → Azure SQL Database
Images · unstructured
Crime-scene photos & maps
Dump-site photography, hand-annotated wall maps, surveillance stills.
→ Blob Storage + AI Vision tagging
Legacy · structured
The VAX export & FBI/VICAP records
The million-record minicomputer dump and cross-jurisdiction homicide data (the future HITS/ViCAP feeds).
→ Data Factory copy → Synapse
Why this matters to your theory Ridgway's name appears in this pile repeatedly — a 1983 suspect report, witness sightings on the strip, employment records showing absences. In the analog world those mentions lived in different boxes in different buildings. Once ingested, they're rows that can be joined on one key: "Gary Leon Ridgway."

04The reference architecture

A standard modern analytics pipeline — the exact shape DP-900 teaches — pointed at a 1982 homicide investigation. Data flows left to right: from messy reality into decisions.

Ingest
Data Factoryorchestration / ETL AI Doc IntelligenceOCR + handwriting
Store (raw)
Data Lake Gen2all paper, scanned Blob Storagephotos / maps
Operate
Azure SQL DBcase mgmt (OLTP) Cosmos DBtip intake
Analyze
Synapsewarehouse (OLAP) Databricks / MLlink + scoring
Act
Power BI + Mapsgeo-profiling AI Searchask anything
DP-900 concept · the analytics workload This is the canonical pattern: ingest → store → process → analyze → visualize. Note the deliberate split between transactional (OLTP) systems that run the day-to-day case, and analytical (OLAP) systems that find patterns across all of it. Confusing those two is the single most common DP-900 exam trap.

05The stack, service by service

Click any service to expand: what it is in DP-900 terms, its job on the case, and specifically how it moves Ridgway from "name in a box" to "man in handcuffs." Each card ends with the exam concept it reinforces.

🏭
Azure Data FactoryIngestion & orchestration
▶

The conveyor belt. Data Factory is a managed ETL/ELT service that runs scheduled pipelines to copy and transform data from many sources into one destination — no servers to babysit.

On the case it runs nightly: pull new tip sheets, motel registries, DMV records, and the VAX export; push scanned paper through Document Intelligence; land everything in the lake.

How it catches him: it dissolves the agency silos. The 1983 King County suspect report and the SeaTac employment records stop being in separate buildings and become rows in the same pipeline, refreshed every night.
DP-900 · data ingestion & ETL vs ELT
🗄️
Data Lake Storage Gen2Raw landing zone
▶

Cheap, bottomless storage for data of any shape — the place you dump everything before you know what you'll ask of it. Structured, semi-structured, unstructured, all welcome.

Here it holds every scanned index card, photo, transcript, and the million-record VAX dump, in original form, forever.

How it catches him: nothing gets thrown away or aged out. The untested spray-paint note and the parked DNA sample metadata stay live and queryable, instead of decaying in an evidence-room box.
DP-900 · structured vs unstructured · data lake
📑
Azure AI Document IntelligenceOCR + handwriting
▶

Reads documents the way a clerk would — printed text, handwriting, tables, and form fields — and turns them into structured data. This is the bridge from paper to database.

It transcribes 10,000+ handwritten tips and field notes into searchable text, extracting fields like date, location, vehicle, and names.

How it catches him: the detective's handwritten note "white/primer pickup, Pac Hwy S" becomes a searchable, joinable record — instead of a scribble in a box no one reopens.
DP-900 · AI workloads · unstructured → structured
⚡
Azure Cosmos DBHigh-volume tip intake (NoSQL)
▶

A globally distributed NoSQL database built for massive write volume and flexible schema. Every tip can have different fields and it doesn't care — it just absorbs them fast.

It's the front door for the public tip flood: the exact load that buried the VAX, now handled with elastic throughput.

How it catches him: no tip is ever "waiting to be entered." The 6,000 unchecked names don't exist as a backlog — they're all in, all indexed, the moment they arrive.
DP-900 · non-relational data · NoSQL use cases
🗃️
Azure SQL DatabaseCase management (OLTP)
▶

A managed relational database for the structured backbone: tables for suspects, victims, vehicles, evidence items, interviews — joined by keys, enforced by constraints.

This is the system of record detectives work in daily: log an interview, update a suspect, link a vehicle to a sighting.

How it catches him: a foreign key does what red string couldn't. "Ridgway" the suspect, the vehicle he owns, and the strip where he was repeatedly seen become one joined entity, not three disconnected files.
DP-900 · relational data · OLTP · keys & normalization
🏢
Azure Synapse AnalyticsData warehouse (OLAP)
▶

The analytical engine. Synapse pulls together huge volumes from the lake and the operational stores to answer big cross-cutting questions fast — the warehouse half of the OLTP/OLAP split.

It runs the queries no detective could run by hand across a million records: every suspect seen on the strip AND absent from work on a disappearance date AND owning a pickup.

How it catches him: this is where the haystack collapses. A single analytical query intersects employment absences, strip sightings, and vehicle type — and Ridgway floats to the top of a very short list.
DP-900 · analytical workloads · OLAP · data warehouse
🔬
Azure Databricks / Azure MLLink analysis & risk scoring
▶

The data-science layer. Spark-based processing for entity resolution (is "G. Ridgway" the same person as "Gary Ridgeway"?), link analysis, and machine-learning models that score and rank.

It builds a suspect-priority model from objective signals: proximity to victim corridors, schedule overlap with disappearances, repeat name mentions across documents.

How it catches him: it neutralizes the polygraph problem. A model doesn't get fooled by a calm demeanor — it weights the absences and sightings Ridgway couldn't talk his way out of, and keeps him near the top instead of letting him drop off the list.
DP-900 · ML basics · classification & clustering
🔎
Azure AI SearchAsk the case anything
▶

An indexing and search service that makes every document — including all the OCR'd paper — instantly full-text searchable, with relevance ranking and filters.

Detectives type a plain-language query and get hits across tips, statements, and notes in milliseconds.

How it catches him: "every document mentioning a primer-spotted pickup near the SeaTac strip" returns Ridgway-linked records in one search — the cross-reference that, in reality, took years and never connected in time.
DP-900 · search & indexing · AI workloads
🗺️
Azure Maps + Power BIGeographic profiling & the decision dashboard
▶

The wall of pins and red string, reborn as live geospatial analytics. Azure Maps geocodes every dump site, sighting, and victim's last-known location; Power BI turns it into an interactive dashboard with heat maps, clustering, and time sliders.

Investigators see dump-site clusters tighten in real time, overlay them on the SeaTac/Pacific Highway corridor, and run geographic profiling to predict the offender's anchor point (home/work).

How it catches him: geographic profiling points at the exact corridor Ridgway lived and worked. Cross-referenced with the suspect list from Synapse, the map stops being decoration and becomes a pointer — narrowing "somewhere in King County" to "this stretch of highway, these few men."
DP-900 · data visualization · Power BI · reporting

06The query that ends it

The same investigative question, asked of 1984 and of the Azure platform. Flip the toggle. This is the difference between "six months per name" and "one query."

QUERY ▸ "Show every suspect seen on the SeaTac strip, who owns a pickup truck, and was absent from work on a victim's disappearance date."

Response time: weeks to months, if ever.

The strip sightings live in detectives' notebooks. The vehicle data is at the DMV. The employment records — if anyone thought to subpoena them — are paper time cards at Kenworth. There is no shared key and no way to join them. A detective would have to manually pull each set, transcribe them, and eyeball the overlap across thousands of names. In reality, this intersection was never run in time. Ridgway stayed on the list but never at the top.

RESULTQuery not executable with available tooling.

Response time: sub-second (Synapse analytical query joining three ingested sources).

RANK 1Gary L. Ridgway — 11 strip sightings · owns Ford pickup · absent on 6 of 9 disappearance dates
RANK 2[suspect B] — 3 sightings · owns pickup · absent on 1 date
RANK 3[suspect C] — 2 sightings · owns pickup · 0 schedule matches

The model surfaces a short, ranked list. Ridgway isn't hidden in 6,000 unchecked names — he's rank one, with the objective signals a polygraph could never override. (Ranking shown is illustrative of how the pipeline would prioritize, not a claim about specific case records.)

07The rewritten timeline

Red markers are what actually happened. Teal markers are where the Azure platform changes the outcome. Your theory, on a clock.

Jul 1982
First victims found in the Green River. The task force forms and begins drowning in paper almost immediately.
1982 — Azure
Day one: tips, statements, and field notes ingested and OCR'd into the lake. Nothing waits in a box. Geographic dashboard goes live with the first dump sites.
1983
Ridgway named as a suspect after Marie Malvar disappears. The lead exists — and gets buried in the volume.
1983 — Azure
That suspect report auto-joins to strip sightings and his vehicle record. The risk model starts ranking him. He does not drop off the list.
1984
Task force buys a $200K VAX; Ridgway passes a polygraph and slides down the priorities.
1984 — Azure
Geographic profiling points at the Pacific Highway corridor — where Ridgway lives and works. The model ignores the polygraph and keeps him at rank one. This is the realistic catch window.
1987
Police collect a saliva sample from Ridgway — then lack the DNA tech to use it. It sits for 14 years.
2001
STR-PCR DNA finally matches the 1987 sample to three early victims. Ridgway is arrested at the Kenworth truck plant on Nov 30.
2001 — Azure
In the reconstruction, the evidence-tracking record flagged that sample as "untestable — re-run when method improves." The match fires automatically the moment STR-PCR exists — but by then he was caught a decade earlier anyway.
The payoff Ridgway's confirmed killing continued into the late 1990s. A realistic 1984–1988 capture window — driven by queryable data, not new evidence — is the difference your theory is built on. Conservatively, that's a dozen or more lives.

08Where the theory is strong, and where it strains

The point of a good case study is to argue your own thesis honestly. Here's the steel-man and the counter.

Strongest ground

The data genuinely existed and genuinely couldn't be cross-referenced. The VAX backlog, the 6,000 unchecked names, the siloed agencies, and the 14-year gap between collecting the DNA sample and being able to read it are all real and all squarely "data platform" problems. Making analog records queryable and joinable is not science fiction — it's exactly what the DP-900 stack does.

Where it strains (say it plainly)

Three honest limits. First, DNA still needs the lab. Azure makes the 1987 sample findable and flagged, but it can't invent STR-PCR — the conviction-grade forensic match still waits on 2001-era chemistry. The platform's contribution is the circumstantial case that gets him arrested and sampled earlier, plus auto-rerunning the match the instant the method exists.

Second, garbage in, garbage out. If a sighting was never written down, no pipeline recovers it. The model is only as good as what detectives actually recorded.

Third, circumstantial ranking isn't proof. Being rank one on a risk model gets you a warrant and surveillance, not a verdict. The honest claim isn't "Azure convicts him in 1984" — it's "Azure puts him under the microscope years earlier, which is where the real evidence trail then accelerates."

For your DP-900 study If you can explain why each service in section 5 belongs to its layer — ingest, store, OLTP, OLAP, ML, viz — and articulate the OLTP-vs-OLAP and structured-vs-unstructured distinctions using this case, you've internalized roughly a third of the exam's conceptual weight. The case is the mnemonic.

A note on tone and accuracy

This is an educational thought experiment built around a real tragedy in which at least 49 women were murdered. Case facts (timeline, victim count, the VAX, the 1987 sample, the 2001 DNA match) are drawn from the public record and cited below. The Azure "catch" scenarios are illustrative reconstructions of how the platform would prioritize information — not claims about specific evidence in the actual case files. The victims were real people; the analytics are hypothetical.

Sources

Gary Ridgway — Wikipedia Green River Killer: A Timeline — Biography.com How the Green River Killer Got Caught — LegalClarity How a crime lab missed evidence — NBC News Green River homicides investigation — King County Casting a Net at Green River — TIME archive Homicide Investigation Tracking System (HITS) Violent Criminal Apprehension Program (ViCAP) How Ridgway was captured with 20-year-old DNA — Oxygen

Project Green River · a DP-900 data-analytics case study · built for Ben Whetstone

The strip sightings live in detectives' notebooks. The vehicle data is at the DMV. The employment records — if anyone thought to subpoena them — are paper time cards at Kenworth. There is no shared key and no way to join them. A detective would have to manually pull each set, transcribe them, and eyeball the overlap across thousands of names. In reality, this intersection was never run in time. Ridgway stayed on the list but never at the top.

RESULTQuery not executable with available tooling.

Response time: sub-second (Synapse analytical query joining three ingested sources).

RANK 1Gary L. Ridgway — 11 strip sightings · owns Ford pickup · absent on 6 of 9 disappearance dates
RANK 2[suspect B] — 3 sightings · owns pickup · absent on 1 date
RANK 3[suspect C] — 2 sightings · owns pickup · 0 schedule matches

The model surfaces a short, ranked list. Ridgway isn't hidden in 6,000 unchecked names — he's rank one, with the objective signals a polygraph could never override. (Ranking shown is illustrative of how the pipeline would prioritize, not a claim about specific case records.)

07The rewritten timeline

Red markers are what actually happened. Teal markers are where the Azure platform changes the outcome. Your theory, on a clock.

Jul 1982
First victims found in the Green River. The task force forms and begins drowning in paper almost immediately.
1982 — Azure
Day one: tips, statements, and field notes ingested and OCR'd into the lake. Nothing waits in a box. Geographic dashboard goes live with the first dump sites.
1983
Ridgway named as a suspect after Marie Malvar disappears. The lead exists — and gets buried in the volume.
1983 — Azure
That suspect report auto-joins to strip sightings and his vehicle record. The risk model starts ranking him. He does not drop off the list.
1984
Task force buys a $200K VAX; Ridgway passes a polygraph and slides down the priorities.
1984 — Azure
Geographic profiling points at the Pacific Highway corridor — where Ridgway lives and works. The model ignores the polygraph and keeps him at rank one. This is the realistic catch window.
1987
Police collect a saliva sample from Ridgway — then lack the DNA tech to use it. It sits for 14 years.
2001
STR-PCR DNA finally matches the 1987 sample to three early victims. Ridgway is arrested at the Kenworth truck plant on Nov 30.
2001 — Azure
In the reconstruction, the evidence-tracking record flagged that sample as "untestable — re-run when method improves." The match fires automatically the moment STR-PCR exists — but by then he was caught a decade earlier anyway.
The payoff Ridgway's confirmed killing continued into the late 1990s. A realistic 1984–1988 capture window — driven by queryable data, not new evidence — is the difference your theory is built on. Conservatively, that's a dozen or more lives.

08Where the theory is strong, and where it strains

The point of a good case study is to argue your own thesis honestly. Here's the steel-man and the counter.

Strongest ground

The data genuinely existed and genuinely couldn't be cross-referenced. The VAX backlog, the 6,000 unchecked names, the siloed agencies, and the 14-year gap between collecting the DNA sample and being able to read it are all real and all squarely "data platform" problems. Making analog records queryable and joinable is not science fiction — it's exactly what the DP-900 stack does.

Where it strains (say it plainly)

Three honest limits. First, DNA still needs the lab. Azure makes the 1987 sample findable and flagged, but it can't invent STR-PCR — the conviction-grade forensic match still waits on 2001-era chemistry. The platform's contribution is the circumstantial case that gets him arrested and sampled earlier, plus auto-rerunning the match the instant the method exists.

Second, garbage in, garbage out. If a sighting was never written down, no pipeline recovers it. The model is only as good as what detectives actually recorded.

Third, circumstantial ranking isn't proof. Being rank one on a risk model gets you a warrant and surveillance, not a verdict. The honest claim isn't "Azure convicts him in 1984" — it's "Azure puts him under the microscope years earlier, which is where the real evidence trail then accelerates."

For your DP-900 study If you can explain why each service in section 5 belongs to its layer — ingest, store, OLTP, OLAP, ML, viz — and articulate the OLTP-vs-OLAP and structured-vs-unstructured distinctions using this case, you've internalized roughly a third of the exam's conceptual weight. The case is the mnemonic.

A note on tone and accuracy

This is an educational thought experiment built around a real tragedy in which at least 49 women were murdered. Case facts (timeline, victim count, the VAX, the 1987 sample, the 2001 DNA match) are drawn from the public record and cited below. The Azure "catch" scenarios are illustrative reconstructions of how the platform would prioritize information — not claims about specific evidence in the actual case files. The victims were real people; the analytics are hypothetical.

Sources

Gary Ridgway — Wikipedia Green River Killer: A Timeline — Biography.com How the Green River Killer Got Caught — LegalClarity How a crime lab missed evidence — NBC News Green River homicides investigation — King County Casting a Net at Green River — TIME archive Homicide Investigation Tracking System (HITS) Violent Criminal Apprehension Program (ViCAP) How Ridgway was captured with 20-year-old DNA — Oxygen

Project Green River · a DP-900 data-analytics case study · built for Ben Whetstone

Based on the search results, here's what happened with Marie Malvar's disappearance and how it led police to Gary Ridgway:

## The Disappearance and Initial Investigation

Marie Malvar was last seen by her boyfriend along Pacific Highway South, climbing into a truck. He became suspicious of this, so he got in his vehicle and tried following it but couldn't find it. It was April 30, 1983, when Marie Malvar's boyfriend, Robert Woods, watched her get into Ridgway's truck, noticing its patches of primer. Woods followed them to a motel parking lot and out again. He lost them at a traffic signal, when Ridgway turned east on South 216th in south Des Moines.

## Leading Police to Ridgway's House

Four days after her disappearance, Marie's boyfriend contacted police, saying he found the truck he tried following parked in front of a house. The house belonged to a man named Gary Ridgway. Police interviewed Ridgway, but he claimed he had nothing to do with Marie's disappearance.

## The Broader Impact

Malvar's disappearance brought Ridgway to official attention and kept him there, even after he passed a 1984 polygraph denying any knowledge of the killings. In 2003, Ridgway confessed to most of the other murders attributed to him, including Marie's.

Based on the web search results, here's information about Gary Ridgway's dump site clusters and the related investigative elements you mentioned:

## Dump Site Clusters and Body Disposal Pattern

Ridgway strangled his victims and dumped bodies in clusters for easy recall, often returning to the sites. After strangling them, he would dump their bodies in forested and overgrown areas, often returning to the bodies to engage in acts of necrophilia. He developed a pattern of picking up victims along Pacific Highway South, strangling them, and dumping their bodies in wooded areas or the Green River.

## Victims and Targeting

The victims were believed to be either prostitutes or runaways, whom he picked up along Pacific Highway South.

## Ted Bundy and Task Force Profile

Task force members included Robert Keppel and Dave Reichert, who periodically interviewed incarcerated serial killer Ted Bundy in 1984. Bundy offered his opinions on the psychology, motivations, and behavior of the killer. He suggested that the killer was revisiting the dump sites to have sex with his victims, which turned out to be true, and if police found a fresh grave, they should stake it out and wait for him to come back.

## Revisited Bodies Pattern

He discarded the bodies in secluded clusters so he could visit them later. This revisitation behavior, predicted by Ted Bundy's profile, proved to be accurate in Ridgway's case.

input.content:
DP-900 Field Project · Data Fundamentals Case Study

Project Green River What if the 1982 task force had Azure instead of a $200,000 minicomputer and a wall of red string? A study of evidence, ingestion, and the cloud services that read it.

Gary Ridgway murdered at least 49 women across Washington and stayed free for nearly twenty years. He was not invisible. A victim's boyfriend wrote down his license plate and led police to his front door in 1983. The signals existed in the evidence. The problem was that the evidence was paper, scattered across agencies, and impossible to query. This is the case rebuilt as a modern Azure data platform, evidence type by evidence type, with the DP-900 exam concept attached to each one.

Theory: caught by ~1988, not 2001 Built for: DP-900 exam prep Format: click any panel to expand
Thesis Exam Map The Data Gap Evidence Dossiers Architecture Service Reference Analytics Ladder Data Roles Query Demo Timeline Caveats

01The thesis

The Green River investigation did not fail for lack of data. It failed because the data was analog, siloed across jurisdictions, and effectively un-searchable. Ridgway was a name in a box of index cards that no one could connect to the other boxes.

49+
confirmed victims
~19
years at large (1982–2001)
$10M+
spent by the task force
6,000
names left unchecked

In 1984 the task force bought a $200,000 VAX minicomputer and spent two years feeding it more than a million records. It was the best technology money could buy, and it still couldn't keep up: roughly 1,300 suspects investigated, ~6,000 more names waiting, each check taking "anywhere from an hour to six months." Detectives covered the walls with maps, pins, and red string and worked 14-hour days canvassing motels along the SeaTac strip. Ridgway sat inside that haystack the entire time.

The core failure The evidence to identify Ridgway was collected in 1983–1987. The technology to read it caught up in 2001. Everything here is about collapsing that 14-year gap — not by inventing evidence, but by making the evidence the investigators already had instantly ingested, joined, searchable, and pattern-aware.

02How this maps to your exam

DP-900 is organized into four big domains. This case study is deliberately built to exercise all four. Use the case as the story you hang the vocabulary on — that's how the abstractions stick.

Core data concepts
Sections 3–4. Structured vs semi-structured vs unstructured; transactional (OLTP) vs analytical (OLAP); the ingest→store→process→analyze→visualize pipeline; ETL vs ELT.
Relational data
Dossier 4. Azure SQL Database, tables, keys, normalization, and why a relational store runs the live case (PaaS vs IaaS included).
Non-relational data
Dossiers 1–3. Cosmos DB, document/NoSQL stores, Blob & Data Lake, throughput, flexible schema for the tip flood.
Analytics & AI
Dossiers 5–6 + Sections 7–8. Synapse, Databricks/ML, Power BI, the AI services (Document Intelligence, Language, Vision, Search), and the four analytics types.

03Then vs. now: the same case, two toolkits

Every real 1980s bottleneck has a cloud-native answer. This table is the whole project in miniature.

● 1982–2001 reality

  • Tips on paper forms and index cards; statements typed or handwritten and filed in boxes.
  • King County, Seattle PD, neighboring jurisdictions, and the FBI each kept their own records in their own formats.
  • A single VAX choking on a million records; cross-referencing one name could take up to six months.
  • Geographic analysis done by hand with pins and string on wall maps.
  • Spray-paint trace sat ~20 years untested; the saliva/DNA sample sat 14 years.
  • Ridgway passed a 1984 polygraph and slid down the priority list.

● Azure reconstruction

  • Every paper artifact OCR'd and handwriting-recognized into a searchable lake (AI Document Intelligence).
  • One unified analytical store ingesting all jurisdictions on a schedule (Data Factory + Data Lake + Synapse).
  • Sub-second full-text search across every note, statement, and tip (Azure AI Search).
  • Automated dump-site clustering and geographic profiling (Azure Maps + Power BI + ML).
  • An evidence record that flags untested items and auto-reruns matches when methods improve.
  • A suspect risk score built on signals a polygraph can't override (Databricks / Azure ML).

04Evidence dossiers: how each type is ingested, stored & analyzed

This is the heart of the project, and the part that matters most for your exam. Each dossier takes one era-appropriate evidence type, tells the real case story attached to it, then walks the full data lifecycle: how it gets ingested, where it's stored and why, how it's analyzed, and the DP-900 concept it teaches. Click to expand.

01
Handwritten field notes & index cardsUnstructured · the richest, least searchable source
▶
The real case

On April 30, 1983, Marie Malvar's boyfriend, Robert Woods, watched her climb into a pickup truck on Pacific Highway South. He noticed its patches of primer paint and followed it until he lost it at a light. Days later he spotted the same truck parked at a house — Gary Ridgway's. He brought police the address. A detective scribbled the lead into notes and an index card. Ridgway denied everything, and the card went into a box with thousands of others. The single most valuable lead in the case became a needle in the file room.

How it's ingested

Each card and notebook page is scanned to an image and dropped into the raw zone. Azure AI Document Intelligence runs its handwriting/OCR model over them, extracting both the free text and structured fields (date, location, vehicle, names) into JSON. Azure Data Factory orchestrates this as a scheduled pipeline: detect new scans, call the model, write results downstream.

AI Document IntelligenceData FactoryData Lake Gen2 (raw)
How it's analyzed

Azure AI Language applies named-entity recognition to pull people, vehicles, and places out of the messy prose. Azure AI Search indexes every transcribed card so a detective can full-text search "primer pickup, Pac Hwy S" and get instant hits — the cross-reference that, in reality, was never made.

AI Language (NER)AI Search
Exam point

This is the classic unstructured data example. It also shows an AI workload turning unstructured input into structured output. Remember the order of the analytics pipeline: ingest → store → process → analyze → visualize. Document Intelligence is "process"; it's what makes unstructured data usable.

What it reveals

The Malvar card stops being a dead end. "Ridgway + primer pickup + Pacific Highway South" becomes a joinable record that links to his vehicle registration and to other sightings — surfacing him years before the DNA ever spoke.

02
Typed witness & victim statementsSemi-structured · consistent fields, free-text bodies
▶
The real case

Many of the women who worked the strip told police they had seen the same ordinary-looking man, repeatedly, in a truck. Robert Woods gave a formal statement. Each statement was a typed form with the same header fields (name, date, location) wrapped around a free-text account. Individually, each was just one more piece of paper. Collectively, they were a chorus naming the same person — but no one could hear the chorus because the pages never sat in one queryable place.

How it's ingested

Typed forms run through Document Intelligence's layout model, which understands form structure: it lifts the header fields into clean key-value pairs and keeps the narrative body as text. The result is a JSON document per statement — predictable fields plus a variable body. That's the definition of semi-structured data.

AI Document Intelligence (layout)Data Factory
How it's stored & analyzed

The JSON documents land in Azure Cosmos DB, a NoSQL document store that's happy with varying schemas. Analysis uses AI Language for entity + key-phrase extraction and AI Search to query across all statements at once: "every statement mentioning a man in a pickup who 'seemed normal.'"

Cosmos DB (document)AI LanguageAI Search
Exam point

Semi-structured data = has some organizational tags/fields but no rigid table schema; JSON is the textbook example. Cosmos DB is Azure's globally distributed NoSQL service for exactly this. Know its model: flexible schema, multiple APIs (NoSQL/Core, MongoDB, Cassandra, Table, Gremlin), low-latency reads/writes.

What it reveals

Frequency. A query across all statements shows the same description recurring — a measurable signal that a manual file review buried. The "ordinary man in the truck" becomes a ranked lead, not an anecdote.

03
Public tip-line sheets (10,000+)High-volume intake · the flood that buried the VAX
▶
The real case

Public attention generated an avalanche of tips. The task force investigated ~1,300 suspects and still had roughly 6,000 names waiting to be checked, with each check taking up to six months. The bottleneck wasn't bad tips — Ridgway's name was in there more than once. The bottleneck was throughput: a single minicomputer and a finite number of detectives could not physically enter, dedupe, and cross-check the volume fast enough.

How it's ingested

Azure Data Factory runs high-volume batch ingestion: forms scanned, OCR'd, and written straight into the store with no human re-keying. There is no "data entry backlog" because ingestion is automated and elastic.

Data Factory (batch)AI Document Intelligence
How it's stored & analyzed

Cosmos DB absorbs the write storm — it scales throughput horizontally and doesn't care that every tip has slightly different fields. Azure Databricks then runs Spark jobs for deduplication and entity resolution: collapsing "G. Ridgway," "Gary Ridgeway," and "Gary L. Ridgway" into one entity and counting how many independent tips point to him.

Cosmos DBDatabricks (Spark)Synapse
Exam point

This is the canonical NoSQL use case: massive, fast, schema-flexible writes. A traditional relational database struggles here; that's why non-relational stores exist. Note the concept of throughput/scale-out (Cosmos provisions capacity in Request Units). Also note: this is ELT (load raw first, transform later in Databricks), not ETL.

What it reveals

Repetition becomes a number. Instead of 6,000 unchecked names, you get a ranked list where Ridgway — named in multiple independent tips — rises near the top automatically, the moment the data lands.

04
Structured records: vehicle, motel & employmentRelational · DMV plates, check-in logs, time cards
▶
The real case

Three structured datasets quietly held the case. The DMV had the pickup registered to Ridgway. Motels along the strip kept check-in registries. And Kenworth, the truck plant where Ridgway worked as a spray painter, had time cards — which, it was later noted, showed him absent on days victims disappeared. Each dataset was clean and tabular. None of them was ever joined to the others in time.

How it's ingested

These are already structured, so Data Factory uses simple scheduled copy activities — connect to the source, map columns, load on a cadence. No OCR needed; this is straightforward ETL into tables.

Data Factory (copy activity)
How it's stored & analyzed

Landed in Azure SQL Database, a managed relational store. Tables for suspects, vehicles, sightings, and employment are linked by keys. A detective updates the live case here (a transactional/OLTP workload). For the big cross-source question, the data flows to Synapse, which joins employment absences × strip sightings × vehicle type across millions of rows.

Azure SQL Database (OLTP)Synapse (OLAP)
Exam point

Pure relational data: fixed schema, rows/columns, primary & foreign keys, normalization. Azure SQL Database is PaaS (Microsoft manages the infrastructure); SQL Server on an Azure VM would be IaaS; SQL Managed Instance is also PaaS. And the key distinction: OLTP (Azure SQL, running the case) vs OLAP (Synapse, analyzing across it). This OLTP/OLAP split is one of the most tested ideas on DP-900.

What it reveals

The join no human ran. A foreign key does what red string couldn't: one analytical query intersecting the three tables puts Ridgway — strip-present, pickup-owning, absent on disappearance dates — at the very top of a very short list.

05
Crime-scene photos & dump-site geographyUnstructured images + spatial data · the pins and string
▶
The real case

Ridgway dumped victims in clusters in wooded areas and the Green River so he could find them again — and he returned to the sites. The task force consulted the imprisoned Ted Bundy, who predicted exactly this revisitation behavior and advised staking out fresh graves. The geographic pattern was real and meaningful, but it lived as pins on a wall map analyzed by eye. The clusters pointed, roughly, at the corridor where Ridgway lived and worked.

How it's ingested

Two streams. Photographs are unstructured binary files, so they go to Azure Blob Storage (object storage), with Azure AI Vision auto-tagging contents and reading any text. Recovery locations are converted to coordinates by Azure Maps geocoding and stored as structured spatial rows.

Blob Storage (images)AI Vision (tagging/OCR)Azure Maps (geocode)
How it's analyzed

Power BI renders the dump sites as a live map with heat-mapping and a time slider — the wall of pins, but queryable. Azure ML runs clustering (e.g., k-means) over the coordinates to perform geographic profiling: estimating the offender's likely anchor point (home/work). Bundy's revisitation insight becomes an automated geofence alert when activity recurs near a known site.

Power BI (geo viz)Azure ML (clustering)Azure Maps
Exam point

Blob Storage = unstructured object storage (know the access tiers: hot/cool/cold/archive). Power BI = the visualization layer (Desktop authors; Service publishes/shares; reports vs dashboards). Clustering is an unsupervised machine learning technique — contrast it with classification, which is supervised. These ML basics are squarely in the analytics/AI domain.

What it reveals

The map stops being decoration and becomes a pointer. Geographic profiling narrows "somewhere in King County" to a specific stretch of Pacific Highway South — the exact corridor of Ridgway's life — and cross-referenced with the Synapse suspect list, the two pointers meet on one man.

06
Forensic & biological evidenceChain-of-custody data · the paint trace & the 1987 sample
▶
The real case

Two pieces of physical evidence damn the timeline. A distinctive spray paint was found on material associated with a victim — and Ridgway was a spray painter at Kenworth — yet the paint went ~20 years untested. And in 1987 police collected a saliva sample from Ridgway, but the DNA science of the day couldn't use it. It sat for 14 years until STR-PCR matched it in 2001. The evidence didn't change. Only our ability to read it did.

How it's ingested & stored

This is about data governance, not OCR. Every physical item gets a record in Azure SQL Database — an evidence table with chain-of-custody, status, and a "testable-with-current-methods" flag. Lab reports and the raw sample metadata are retained in the Data Lake indefinitely (storage is cheap; never discard raw data).

Azure SQL (evidence register)Data Lake (lab reports)
How it's analyzed & acted on

A Data Factory pipeline watches for new capabilities: when a more sensitive method comes online, it automatically re-queues every item flagged "untestable" for re-analysis and alerts the team on a match — instead of relying on someone to remember a sample from 14 years ago. The spray-paint trace, meanwhile, is a structured attribute that joins straight to Ridgway's Kenworth employment record.

Data Factory (trigger/re-run)Synapse (join to employment)
Exam point

Two themes: data governance & the value of retaining raw data (you can re-process the lake later as tools improve), and the full data analytics lifecycle closing the loop into action. Honest scope note for accuracy: the actual DNA chemistry happens in a lab, not in Azure — the platform's job is to track, flag, join, and auto-trigger, not to invent forensics.

What it reveals

The paint trace links a victim directly to Ridgway's occupation years early. And the parked sample never gets forgotten — the moment STR-PCR exists, the match fires on its own. In the rewritten timeline, though, the circumstantial platform has already put him under surveillance long before.

05The reference architecture

All six dossiers feed one pipeline — the exact shape DP-900 teaches. Data flows left to right, from messy reality into decisions.

Ingest
Data Factoryorchestration / ETL · ELTAI Doc IntelligenceOCR + handwriting
Store (raw)
Data Lake Gen2all paper, scannedBlob Storagephotos / maps
Operate
Azure SQL DBcase mgmt (OLTP)Cosmos DBtips/statements (NoSQL)
Analyze
Synapsewarehouse (OLAP)Databricks / MLlink + scoring
Act
Power BI + Mapsgeo-profilingAI Searchask anything
DP-900 concept · the analytics workload Memorize this flow: ingest → store → process → analyze → visualize. And keep the two worlds straight: transactional (OLTP) systems run the day-to-day case; analytical (OLAP) systems find patterns across all of it. Mixing those up is the single most common DP-900 trap.

06Service quick-reference

A flash-card style summary of every service in the stack: what it is, and its one-line job on the case. Good for last-minute review.

🏭
Azure Data FactoryIngestion & orchestration

Managed, serverless ETL/ELT. Scheduled pipelines copy & transform data from many sources into one place.

CASE JOB · Dissolves the agency silos — nightly ingest of tips, registries, DMV, VAX export.
🗄️
Data Lake Storage Gen2Raw landing zone

Bottomless, cheap storage for any data shape. Built on Blob with a hierarchical namespace. Schema-on-read.

CASE JOB · Nothing ages out — paper scans & raw samples stay queryable forever.
📑
AI Document IntelligenceOCR + handwriting

Reads printed text, handwriting, forms & tables; outputs structured data. (Formerly Form Recognizer.)

CASE JOB · Paper → database — turns 10,000 handwritten tips into searchable records.
⚡
Azure Cosmos DBNoSQL / non-relational

Globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL. Flexible schema, low latency, scale-out throughput (RUs).

CASE JOB · Absorbs the tip flood — no entry backlog, ever.
🗃️
Azure SQL DatabaseRelational · PaaS · OLTP

Managed relational DB. Tables, keys, constraints. PaaS (vs SQL-on-VM = IaaS).

CASE JOB · Runs the live case — suspects, vehicles, evidence, joined by keys.
🏢
Azure Synapse AnalyticsData warehouse · OLAP

Big-data analytics: SQL pools (dedicated/serverless) + Spark + pipelines for cross-cutting queries.

CASE JOB · Collapses the haystack — joins absences × sightings × vehicle in one query.
🔬
Azure Databricks / Azure MLData science & ML

Spark-based processing; build/train/deploy ML models. Clustering (unsupervised) & classification (supervised).

CASE JOB · Neutralizes the polygraph — objective risk scoring & entity resolution.
🔎
Azure AI SearchIndexing & query

Full-text search with relevance ranking, filters, and AI enrichment over your content.

CASE JOB · Ask the case anything — instant search across all OCR'd documents.
🗺️
Azure MapsGeospatial services

Geocoding, routing, and spatial APIs for location data.

CASE JOB · Turns sites into coordinates — feeds clustering & the map.
📊
Power BIVisualization & reporting

Authoring in Desktop; sharing in the Service. Reports, dashboards, datasets.

CASE JOB · The decision dashboard — heat maps, time sliders, ranked leads.

07The four types of analytics, on one case

DP-900 explicitly tests the analytics ladder: descriptive → diagnostic → predictive → prescriptive (each answers a harder question than the last). Here's the whole ladder told through the investigation.

Descriptive
What happened?
49 victims, mapped by where and when they were found. The basic dashboard.
→ Power BI
Diagnostic
Why did it happen here?
Dump sites cluster along one corridor because that's the offender's comfort zone.
→ Synapse + Maps
Predictive
What's likely next / who?
Geographic profiling estimates the anchor point; the risk model ranks suspects.
→ Azure ML
Prescriptive
What should we do?
Surveil this suspect; stake out the fresh dump site (Bundy's exact advice).
→ ML + alerts
DP-900 concept · analytics typesEach rung is more valuable and harder than the one below it. Descriptive and diagnostic look backward; predictive and prescriptive look forward. If an exam question asks "which type recommends an action," the answer is prescriptive.

08Who builds this — the data roles

DP-900 also tests the three core data roles. The task force, re-staffed for the cloud, would need all three.

Data Engineer
Builds and runs the ingestion pipelines: Data Factory, the lake, OCR processing, the Synapse warehouse. The person who makes the paper flow into the platform.
Database Administrator
Manages the operational case database (Azure SQL): security, backups, access, performance. Guards the chain-of-custody and who can see what.
Data Analyst
Builds the Power BI dashboards and the suspect leaderboard, turning the warehouse into the maps and ranked lists detectives actually act on.

09The query that ends it

The same investigative question, asked of 1984 and of the Azure platform. Flip the toggle — this is the difference between "six months per name" and "one query."

QUERY ▸ "Every suspect seen on the SeaTac strip, who owns a pickup truck, and was absent from work on a victim's disappearance date."

Response time: weeks to months, if ever.

The strip sightings live in detectives' notebooks. The vehicle data is at the DMV. The employment records — if anyone thought to subpoena them — are paper time cards at Kenworth. There is no shared key and no way to join them. A detective would have to pull each set by hand, transcribe them, and eyeball the overlap across thousands of names. In reality, this intersection was never run in time. Ridgway stayed on the list but never at the top.

RESULTQuery not executable with available tooling.

Response time: sub-second (Synapse analytical query joining three ingested sources).

RANK 1Gary L. Ridgway — 11 strip sightings · owns primer-patched pickup · absent on 6 of 9 disappearance dates
RANK 2[suspect B] — 3 sightings · owns pickup · absent on 1 date
RANK 3[suspect C] — 2 sightings · owns pickup · 0 schedule matches

A short, ranked list. Ridgway isn't hidden among 6,000 unchecked names — he's rank one, with the objective signals a polygraph could never override. (Ranking is illustrative of how the pipeline would prioritize, not a claim about specific case records.)

10The rewritten timeline

Red markers are what actually happened. Teal markers are where the Azure platform changes the outcome.

Jul 1982
First victims found in the Green River. The task force forms and begins drowning in paper almost immediately.
1982 — Azure
Day one: tips, statements, and field notes ingested and OCR'd into the lake. Nothing waits in a box. The geographic dashboard goes live with the first dump sites.
Apr 1983
Marie Malvar vanishes. Her boyfriend follows the primer-patched truck, finds it at Ridgway's house, and gives police the address. Ridgway denies it; the lead is buried in the volume.
1983 — Azure
The Malvar lead auto-joins to strip sightings and Ridgway's vehicle record. The risk model starts ranking him. He does not drop off the list.
1984
Task force buys a $200K VAX; consults Ted Bundy on the offender profile; Ridgway passes a polygraph and slides down the priorities.
1984 — Azure
Geographic profiling points at the Pacific Highway corridor — where Ridgway lives and works. The model ignores the polygraph and holds him at rank one. This is the realistic catch window.
1987
Police collect a saliva sample from Ridgway — then lack the DNA tech to use it. It sits for 14 years.
2001
STR-PCR DNA finally matches the 1987 sample to early victims. Ridgway is arrested at the Kenworth truck plant on Nov 30.
2001 — Azure
In the reconstruction, the evidence record flagged that sample "re-run when method improves," so the match fires automatically — but by then he was caught a decade earlier anyway.
The payoffRidgway's confirmed killing continued into the late 1990s. A realistic 1984–1988 capture window — driven by queryable data, not new evidence — is the difference your theory is built on. Conservatively, that's a dozen or more lives.

11Where the theory is strong, and where it strains

A good case study argues its own thesis honestly. Here's the steel-man and the counter.

Strongest ground

The data genuinely existed and genuinely couldn't be cross-referenced. The VAX backlog, the 6,000 unchecked names, the siloed agencies, the Malvar lead delivered to police in 1983, and the 14-year gap between collecting the sample and reading it are all real, and all squarely "data platform" problems. Making analog records queryable and joinable isn't science fiction — it's exactly what the DP-900 stack does.

Where it strains — say it plainly

Three honest limits. First, DNA still needs the lab. Azure makes the 1987 sample findable and flagged, but it can't invent STR-PCR — the conviction-grade match still waits on 2001-era chemistry. The platform's contribution is the circumstantial case that gets him arrested and sampled earlier, plus auto-rerunning the match when the method exists.

Second, garbage in, garbage out. If a sighting was never written down, no pipeline recovers it. The model is only as good as what detectives actually recorded.

Third, circumstantial ranking isn't proof. Being rank one on a risk model earns a warrant and surveillance, not a verdict. The honest claim isn't "Azure convicts him in 1984" — it's "Azure puts him under the microscope years earlier, which is where the real evidence trail accelerates."

For your DP-900 studyIf you can explain why each service sits in its pipeline layer — ingest, store, OLTP, OLAP, ML, viz — and articulate OLTP-vs-OLAP, relational-vs-non-relational, structured-vs-unstructured, and the four analytics types using this case, you've internalized a large share of the exam's conceptual weight. The case is the mnemonic.

A note on tone and accuracy

This is an educational thought experiment built around a real tragedy in which at least 49 women were murdered. Case facts (the timeline, victim count, the VAX, the Malvar lead, the 1987 sample, the 2001 DNA match, Bundy's consult) are drawn from the public record and cited below. The Azure "catch" scenarios are illustrative reconstructions of how the platform would prioritize information — not claims about specific evidence in the actual case files. The victims were real people; the analytics are hypothetical.

Sources

Gary Ridgway — Wikipedia Green River Killer: A Timeline — Biography.com How the Green River Killer Got Caught — LegalClarity How a crime lab missed evidence — NBC News Victim's family had led police to Ridgway's door — HeraldNet Green River homicides investigation — King County Casting a Net at Green River — TIME archive Homicide Investigation Tracking System (HITS) Violent Criminal Apprehension Program (ViCAP) Captured with 20-year-old DNA — Oxygen

DP-900 Field Project · Data Fundamentals Case Study

Project Green River What if the 1982 task force had Azure instead of a $200,000 minicomputer and a wall of red string? A study of evidence, ingestion, and the cloud services that read it.

Gary Ridgway murdered at least 49 women across Washington and stayed free for nearly twenty years. He was not invisible. A victim's boyfriend wrote down his license plate and led police to his front door in 1983. The signals existed in the evidence. The problem was that the evidence was paper, scattered across agencies, and impossible to query. This is the case rebuilt as a modern Azure data platform, evidence type by evidence type, with the DP-900 exam concept attached to each one.

Theory: caught by ~1988, not 2001 Built for: DP-900 exam prep Format: click any panel to expand
Thesis Exam Map The Data Gap Evidence Dossiers Architecture Service Reference Analytics Ladder Data Roles Query Demo Timeline Caveats

01The thesis

The Green River investigation did not fail for lack of data. It failed because the data was analog, siloed across jurisdictions, and effectively un-searchable. Ridgway was a name in a box of index cards that no one could connect to the other boxes.

49+
confirmed victims
~19
years at large (1982–2001)
$10M+
spent by the task force
6,000
names left unchecked

In 1984 the task force bought a $200,000 VAX minicomputer and spent two years feeding it more than a million records. It was the best technology money could buy, and it still couldn't keep up: roughly 1,300 suspects investigated, ~6,000 more names waiting, each check taking "anywhere from an hour to six months." Detectives covered the walls with maps, pins, and red string and worked 14-hour days canvassing motels along the SeaTac strip. Ridgway sat inside that haystack the entire time.

The core failure The evidence to identify Ridgway was collected in 1983–1987. The technology to read it caught up in 2001. Everything here is about collapsing that 14-year gap — not by inventing evidence, but by making the evidence the investigators already had instantly ingested, joined, searchable, and pattern-aware.

02How this maps to your exam

DP-900 is organized into four big domains. This case study is deliberately built to exercise all four. Use the case as the story you hang the vocabulary on — that's how the abstractions stick.

Core data concepts
Sections 3–4. Structured vs semi-structured vs unstructured; transactional (OLTP) vs analytical (OLAP); the ingest→store→process→analyze→visualize pipeline; ETL vs ELT.
Relational data
Dossier 4. Azure SQL Database, tables, keys, normalization, and why a relational store runs the live case (PaaS vs IaaS included).
Non-relational data
Dossiers 1–3. Cosmos DB, document/NoSQL stores, Blob & Data Lake, throughput, flexible schema for the tip flood.
Analytics & AI
Dossiers 5–6 + Sections 7–8. Synapse, Databricks/ML, Power BI, the AI services (Document Intelligence, Language, Vision, Search), and the four analytics types.

03Then vs. now: the same case, two toolkits

Every real 1980s bottleneck has a cloud-native answer. This table is the whole project in miniature.

● 1982–2001 reality

  • Tips on paper forms and index cards; statements typed or handwritten and filed in boxes.
  • King County, Seattle PD, neighboring jurisdictions, and the FBI each kept their own records in their own formats.
  • A single VAX choking on a million records; cross-referencing one name could take up to six months.
  • Geographic analysis done by hand with pins and string on wall maps.
  • Spray-paint trace sat ~20 years untested; the saliva/DNA sample sat 14 years.
  • Ridgway passed a 1984 polygraph and slid down the priority list.

● Azure reconstruction

  • Every paper artifact OCR'd and handwriting-recognized into a searchable lake (AI Document Intelligence).
  • One unified analytical store ingesting all jurisdictions on a schedule (Data Factory + Data Lake + Synapse).
  • Sub-second full-text search across every note, statement, and tip (Azure AI Search).
  • Automated dump-site clustering and geographic profiling (Azure Maps + Power BI + ML).
  • An evidence record that flags untested items and auto-reruns matches when methods improve.
  • A suspect risk score built on signals a polygraph can't override (Databricks / Azure ML).

04Evidence dossiers: how each type is ingested, stored & analyzed

This is the heart of the project, and the part that matters most for your exam. Each dossier takes one era-appropriate evidence type, tells the real case story attached to it, then walks the full data lifecycle: how it gets ingested, where it's stored and why, how it's analyzed, and the DP-900 concept it teaches. Click to expand.

01
Handwritten field notes & index cardsUnstructured · the richest, least searchable source
▶
The real case

On April 30, 1983, Marie Malvar's boyfriend, Robert Woods, watched her climb into a pickup truck on Pacific Highway South. He noticed its patches of primer paint and followed it until he lost it at a light. Days later he spotted the same truck parked at a house — Gary Ridgway's. He brought police the address. A detective scribbled the lead into notes and an index card. Ridgway denied everything, and the card went into a box with thousands of others. The single most valuable lead in the case became a needle in the file room.

How it's ingested

Each card and notebook page is scanned to an image and dropped into the raw zone. Azure AI Document Intelligence runs its handwriting/OCR model over them, extracting both the free text and structured fields (date, location, vehicle, names) into JSON. Azure Data Factory orchestrates this as a scheduled pipeline: detect new scans, call the model, write results downstream.

AI Document IntelligenceData FactoryData Lake Gen2 (raw)
How it's analyzed

Azure AI Language applies named-entity recognition to pull people, vehicles, and places out of the messy prose. Azure AI Search indexes every transcribed card so a detective can full-text search "primer pickup, Pac Hwy S" and get instant hits — the cross-reference that, in reality, was never made.

AI Language (NER)AI Search
Exam point

This is the classic unstructured data example. It also shows an AI workload turning unstructured input into structured output. Remember the order of the analytics pipeline: ingest → store → process → analyze → visualize. Document Intelligence is "process"; it's what makes unstructured data usable.

What it reveals

The Malvar card stops being a dead end. "Ridgway + primer pickup + Pacific Highway South" becomes a joinable record that links to his vehicle registration and to other sightings — surfacing him years before the DNA ever spoke.

02
Typed witness & victim statementsSemi-structured · consistent fields, free-text bodies
▶
The real case

Many of the women who worked the strip told police they had seen the same ordinary-looking man, repeatedly, in a truck. Robert Woods gave a formal statement. Each statement was a typed form with the same header fields (name, date, location) wrapped around a free-text account. Individually, each was just one more piece of paper. Collectively, they were a chorus naming the same person — but no one could hear the chorus because the pages never sat in one queryable place.

How it's ingested

Typed forms run through Document Intelligence's layout model, which understands form structure: it lifts the header fields into clean key-value pairs and keeps the narrative body as text. The result is a JSON document per statement — predictable fields plus a variable body. That's the definition of semi-structured data.

AI Document Intelligence (layout)Data Factory
How it's stored & analyzed

The JSON documents land in Azure Cosmos DB, a NoSQL document store that's happy with varying schemas. Analysis uses AI Language for entity + key-phrase extraction and AI Search to query across all statements at once: "every statement mentioning a man in a pickup who 'seemed normal.'"

Cosmos DB (document)AI LanguageAI Search
Exam point

Semi-structured data = has some organizational tags/fields but no rigid table schema; JSON is the textbook example. Cosmos DB is Azure's globally distributed NoSQL service for exactly this. Know its model: flexible schema, multiple APIs (NoSQL/Core, MongoDB, Cassandra, Table, Gremlin), low-latency reads/writes.

What it reveals

Frequency. A query across all statements shows the same description recurring — a measurable signal that a manual file review buried. The "ordinary man in the truck" becomes a ranked lead, not an anecdote.

03
Public tip-line sheets (10,000+)High-volume intake · the flood that buried the VAX
▶
The real case

Public attention generated an avalanche of tips. The task force investigated ~1,300 suspects and still had roughly 6,000 names waiting to be checked, with each check taking up to six months. The bottleneck wasn't bad tips — Ridgway's name was in there more than once. The bottleneck was throughput: a single minicomputer and a finite number of detectives could not physically enter, dedupe, and cross-check the volume fast enough.

How it's ingested

Azure Data Factory runs high-volume batch ingestion: forms scanned, OCR'd, and written straight into the store with no human re-keying. There is no "data entry backlog" because ingestion is automated and elastic.

Data Factory (batch)AI Document Intelligence
How it's stored & analyzed

Cosmos DB absorbs the write storm — it scales throughput horizontally and doesn't care that every tip has slightly different fields. Azure Databricks then runs Spark jobs for deduplication and entity resolution: collapsing "G. Ridgway," "Gary Ridgeway," and "Gary L. Ridgway" into one entity and counting how many independent tips point to him.

Cosmos DBDatabricks (Spark)Synapse
Exam point

This is the canonical NoSQL use case: massive, fast, schema-flexible writes. A traditional relational database struggles here; that's why non-relational stores exist. Note the concept of throughput/scale-out (Cosmos provisions capacity in Request Units). Also note: this is ELT (load raw first, transform later in Databricks), not ETL.

What it reveals

Repetition becomes a number. Instead of 6,000 unchecked names, you get a ranked list where Ridgway — named in multiple independent tips — rises near the top automatically, the moment the data lands.

04
Structured records: vehicle, motel & employmentRelational · DMV plates, check-in logs, time cards
▶
The real case

Three structured datasets quietly held the case. The DMV had the pickup registered to Ridgway. Motels along the strip kept check-in registries. And Kenworth, the truck plant where Ridgway worked as a spray painter, had time cards — which, it was later noted, showed him absent on days victims disappeared. Each dataset was clean and tabular. None of them was ever joined to the others in time.

How it's ingested

These are already structured, so Data Factory uses simple scheduled copy activities — connect to the source, map columns, load on a cadence. No OCR needed; this is straightforward ETL into tables.

Data Factory (copy activity)
How it's stored & analyzed

Landed in Azure SQL Database, a managed relational store. Tables for suspects, vehicles, sightings, and employment are linked by keys. A detective updates the live case here (a transactional/OLTP workload). For the big cross-source question, the data flows to Synapse, which joins employment absences × strip sightings × vehicle type across millions of rows.

Azure SQL Database (OLTP)Synapse (OLAP)
Exam point

Pure relational data: fixed schema, rows/columns, primary & foreign keys, normalization. Azure SQL Database is PaaS (Microsoft manages the infrastructure); SQL Server on an Azure VM would be IaaS; SQL Managed Instance is also PaaS. And the key distinction: OLTP (Azure SQL, running the case) vs OLAP (Synapse, analyzing across it). This OLTP/OLAP split is one of the most tested ideas on DP-900.

What it reveals

The join no human ran. A foreign key does what red string couldn't: one analytical query intersecting the three tables puts Ridgway — strip-present, pickup-owning, absent on disappearance dates — at the very top of a very short list.

05
Crime-scene photos & dump-site geographyUnstructured images + spatial data · the pins and string
▶
The real case

Ridgway dumped victims in clusters in wooded areas and the Green River so he could find them again — and he returned to the sites. The task force consulted the imprisoned Ted Bundy, who predicted exactly this revisitation behavior and advised staking out fresh graves. The geographic pattern was real and meaningful, but it lived as pins on a wall map analyzed by eye. The clusters pointed, roughly, at the corridor where Ridgway lived and worked.

How it's ingested

Two streams. Photographs are unstructured binary files, so they go to Azure Blob Storage (object storage), with Azure AI Vision auto-tagging contents and reading any text. Recovery locations are converted to coordinates by Azure Maps geocoding and stored as structured spatial rows.

Blob Storage (images)AI Vision (tagging/OCR)Azure Maps (geocode)
How it's analyzed

Power BI renders the dump sites as a live map with heat-mapping and a time slider — the wall of pins, but queryable. Azure ML runs clustering (e.g., k-means) over the coordinates to perform geographic profiling: estimating the offender's likely anchor point (home/work). Bundy's revisitation insight becomes an automated geofence alert when activity recurs near a known site.

Power BI (geo viz)Azure ML (clustering)Azure Maps
Exam point

Blob Storage = unstructured object storage (know the access tiers: hot/cool/cold/archive). Power BI = the visualization layer (Desktop authors; Service publishes/shares; reports vs dashboards). Clustering is an unsupervised machine learning technique — contrast it with classification, which is supervised. These ML basics are squarely in the analytics/AI domain.

What it reveals

The map stops being decoration and becomes a pointer. Geographic profiling narrows "somewhere in King County" to a specific stretch of Pacific Highway South — the exact corridor of Ridgway's life — and cross-referenced with the Synapse suspect list, the two pointers meet on one man.

06
Forensic & biological evidenceChain-of-custody data · the paint trace & the 1987 sample
▶
The real case

Two pieces of physical evidence damn the timeline. A distinctive spray paint was found on material associated with a victim — and Ridgway was a spray painter at Kenworth — yet the paint went ~20 years untested. And in 1987 police collected a saliva sample from Ridgway, but the DNA science of the day couldn't use it. It sat for 14 years until STR-PCR matched it in 2001. The evidence didn't change. Only our ability to read it did.

How it's ingested & stored

This is about data governance, not OCR. Every physical item gets a record in Azure SQL Database — an evidence table with chain-of-custody, status, and a "testable-with-current-methods" flag. Lab reports and the raw sample metadata are retained in the Data Lake indefinitely (storage is cheap; never discard raw data).

Azure SQL (evidence register)Data Lake (lab reports)
How it's analyzed & acted on

A Data Factory pipeline watches for new capabilities: when a more sensitive method comes online, it automatically re-queues every item flagged "untestable" for re-analysis and alerts the team on a match — instead of relying on someone to remember a sample from 14 years ago. The spray-paint trace, meanwhile, is a structured attribute that joins straight to Ridgway's Kenworth employment record.

Data Factory (trigger/re-run)Synapse (join to employment)
Exam point

Two themes: data governance & the value of retaining raw data (you can re-process the lake later as tools improve), and the full data analytics lifecycle closing the loop into action. Honest scope note for accuracy: the actual DNA chemistry happens in a lab, not in Azure — the platform's job is to track, flag, join, and auto-trigger, not to invent forensics.

What it reveals

The paint trace links a victim directly to Ridgway's occupation years early. And the parked sample never gets forgotten — the moment STR-PCR exists, the match fires on its own. In the rewritten timeline, though, the circumstantial platform has already put him under surveillance long before.

05The reference architecture

All six dossiers feed one pipeline — the exact shape DP-900 teaches. Data flows left to right, from messy reality into decisions.

Store (raw)

Azure SQL DBcase mgmt (OLTP)Cosmos DBtips/statements (NoSQL)
Analyze
Synapsewarehouse (OLAP)Databricks / MLlink + scoring
Act
Power BI + Mapsgeo-profilingAI Searchask anything
DP-900 concept · the analytics workload Memorize this flow: ingest → store → process → analyze → visualize. And keep the two worlds straight: transactional (OLTP) systems run the day-to-day case; analytical (OLAP) systems find patterns across all of it. Mixing those up is the single most common DP-900 trap.
